sections.heading: Why linking words matter
sections.body: Without connectors, ideas sit next to each other with no clear relationship. Linking words show the reader how ideas connect — whether one causes another, contrasts with it, or adds to it.

sections.heading: And — addition
sections.body: 'And' adds information of equal weight. It joins clauses, phrases, and single words. Don't start every sentence with 'and' in formal writing, but it's natural and effective in speech.

sections.heading: But — contrast
sections.body: 'But' introduces a contrast or unexpected result. It's the most common adversative connector in English. 'However' is its formal equivalent when starting a sentence.

sections.heading: So — result
sections.body: 'So' introduces a result or consequence. 'Therefore' and 'as a result' are its formal equivalents. Note: 'so' in conversation often functions as a discourse marker too.

sections.heading: Because — reason
sections.body: 'Because' introduces the reason for something. It answers 'why?' It must connect two clauses. 'Because of' is followed by a noun phrase: 'because of the rain' (not 'because the rain').

examples.english: I was tired, but I finished the report.
It rained, so we cancelled the trip.
She studied hard because she wanted to pass.
We stayed in because of the weather.

exercises.instruction: Connect the two sentences using and, but, so, or because.
items: I was hungry. I made a sandwich.
She likes jazz. She doesn't like classical music.
He forgot his wallet. He couldn't pay.
We left early. We wanted to avoid traffic.
answers: I was hungry, so I made a sandwich.
She likes jazz but she doesn't like classical music.
He forgot his wallet, so he couldn't pay.
We left early because we wanted to avoid traffic.

quiz.question: Which sentence uses 'because' correctly?
options: I stayed home because of I was sick.
I stayed home because the rain.
I stayed home because I was sick.
I stayed home because of I felt ill.
quiz.explanation: 'Because' is followed by a full clause (subject + verb). 'Because of' is followed by a noun phrase.
